abstract: Since the introduction of Provincial Reconstruction Teams (PRTs) in Afghanistan, many nongovernmental organizations (NGOs) have argued that blending military and humanitarian missions creates "blurred lines" that jeopardize aid worker safety. These organizations contend that such overlap prevents insurgents from distinguishing between combatants and civilians, making humanitarian staff more frequent targets of attack. Despite the prevalence of this claim in policy circles, empirical validation has remained limited. This study evaluates the impact of PRT presence on NGO security using a panel-corrected standard error regression model covering all 34 Afghan provinces during 2010 and 2011. While initial findings indicate that provinces with PRTs experienced a higher volume of security incidents, a more nuanced analysis reveals that this correlation applies only to teams led by coalition partners other than the United States. In contrast, US-led PRTs did not significantly correlate with increased NGO insecurity. These results challenge the "blurred lines" narrative as a universal explanation for aid worker vulnerability and suggest that the specific operational approaches of different lead nations may play a more decisive role than the mere integration of military and relief work.
